channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Motherhood Changed Ilana Glazer. A Reporter Knew What That Felt Like.
item.description: Sometimes a reporter and subject can converse on a common ground.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Aundre Larrow for The New York Times

item.title: Last-Minute Donation Could Upend G.O.P. Primary for Texas Attorney General
item.description: A single donor’s contribution of $2.75 million could help propel Representative Chip Roy in his clash with a self-funded state senator, Mayes Middleton.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy

item.title: Trump Is Said to Have Proposed a New Helipad at the White House
item.description: The exhaust from helicopters’ engines has been known to occasionally scorch the grass on their landing zones.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Minho Kim
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump walking to board Marine One on the South Lawn of the White House earlier this month.

item.title: A Fearful Phone Call and Frantic Search Preceded San Diego Mosque Shooting
item.description: San Diego police arrived to find three people dead. Nearby, they found two teenagers, the attackers, dead in a car. The violence shattered an idyllic Southern California city.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango, Christina Morales, Candice Reed and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.category: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers raced around San Diego before converging at the mosque.

item.title: Justices Hint at Strains as Supreme Court Comes Under Scrutiny
item.description: Traveling across the country, justices defend the role of the court even as strained relations among its members emerge in writing and remarks.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow and Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson of the Supreme Court talks with Judge Richard Gergel of the U.S. District Court in South Carolina during a fireside chat hosted by the American Law Institute in Washington on Monday.

item.title: Colorado Supreme Court Orders Hospital to Restart Care for Transgender Minors
item.description: Children’s Hospital Colorado had halted hormone therapy and puberty blockers for transgender youths amid threats from the Trump administration to withhold funding from institutions that provide it.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Chet Strange/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Ralph L. Carr Colorado Judicial Center, home of the Colorado Supreme Court in Denver.

item.title: Trump Moves to Admit 10,000 More White South Africans as Refugees
item.description: The Trump administration argues that an “emergency refugee situation” in South Africa merits bringing more Afrikaners to America, at a cost of some $100 million.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and his aides have claimed that white South Africans face racial persecution, an assertion strongly disputed by officials in that country.

item.title: San Diego Mosque Shooting Comes Amid Rising Reports of Islamophobia in the U.S.
item.description: The Council on American-Islamic Relations said complaints about bias against Muslims are at levels unseen in decades.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: Law enforcement officers swarmed to the scene of a shooting at the Islamic Center in San Diego on Monday.

item.title: San Diego Mosque Shooting Shatters a Neighborhood’s Peace
item.description: First came a series of pops, then a race to render aid. It was too late.
item.dc:creator: Candice Reed
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: Women hug as they wait for the children to walk out of the mosque, at a collecting area, at the scene of a shooting at the Islamic Center in San Diego, California on Monday.

item.title: Dallas World Cup Mural: Artist Wyland Says Beloved Whale Artwork Was Painted Over
item.description: The artwork’s removal ahead of the World Cup has ignited a backlash over public art and what cities are willing to sacrifice for global events.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Julio Cortez/Associated Press
item.media:description: The mural “Ocean Life” in downtown Dallas that is being painted over in preparation for the World Cup was dedicated in 1999.

item.title: What to Know About San Diego’s Islamic Center
item.description: A visitor said that the mosque is highly diverse. Its services are in English, because attendees come from all over the world.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: People embracing near the scene of a shooting outside the Islamic Center of San Diego on Monday.

item.title: Why Josh Shapiro Isn’t Acting Like Other Potential 2028 Candidates
item.description: The Pennsylvania governor’s in-state focus is a contrast with other possible presidential hopefuls.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gov. Josh Shapiro of Pennsylvania.

item.title: Nancy Pelosi Endorses Connie Chan to Succeed Her
item.description: Representative Nancy Pelosi, the first woman to serve as speaker of the House, broke her silence on Monday about the race to succeed her and endorsed Connie Chan, a San Francisco official.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nancy Pelosi at a dinner honoring her legacy at the state Democratic Party convention in San Francisco earlier this year.

item.title: Harvard Asks Federal Judge to Dismiss Trump Lawsuit
item.description: The Trump administration sued Harvard earlier this year over allegations it had not done enough to combat antisemitism on campus.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Harvard University in March. Officials at Harvard have strongly objected to the government’s accusations that is not protecting Jews on campus, arguing that it has made improvements.

item.title: Mark Fuhrman, Flawed Witness in O.J. Simpson Trial, Dies at 74
item.description: A Los Angeles police detective, he was discredited during Mr. Simpson’s 1995 murder trial by defense lawyers who pointed to his past use of racist language.
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:credit: Ken Lubas/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Mark Fuhrman on the witness stand during the 1995 murder trial of O.J. Simpson. He was called as a star witness for the prosecution but was thoroughly discredited by Mr. Simpson’s lawyers.

item.title: Channel Islands Fire Started Accidentally by Shipwrecked Sailor
item.description: The shipwrecked man was rescued from Channel Islands National Park, but the flares set off a 14,600-acre fire that has burned park buildings, forced evacuations and threatened rare Torrey pines.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla

item.title: Trump Threatens Iran and Then Pulls Back, All in the Same Day
item.description: President Trump has repeatedly said he’ll restart military action against Iran, only to stop short of plunging the United States directly back into an unpopular war.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jack Taylor/Reuters
item.media:description: A U.S. military official said that Iran had been able to study the flight patterns of American fighter jets and bombers.

item.title: Citing Ebola Outbreak, U.S. Restricts Entry From Congo, Uganda and South Sudan
item.description: The order is in effect for 30 days and does not apply to American citizens or U.S. service members.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Maggie Haberman
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Richard Perry/The New York Times
item.media:description: A Customs and Border Protection officer at John F. Kennedy International Airport in New York.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.description: The police said that the threat had been “neutralized,” but did not confirm the report that the attack had been fatal.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango, Neil Vigdor and Pooja Salhotra

item.title: Sandy Fire in Simi Valley Threatens Thousands of Homes
item.description: Officials ordered more than 29,000 to evacuate after a brush fire broke out in Simi Valley, Calif.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Ethan Swope/Associated Press
item.media:description: The blaze, called the Sandy fire, broke out on a warm, breezy day in Simi Valley.

item.title: American Hantavirus Patient Ordered to Stay in Quarantine Despite Plans to Leave
item.description: A woman from the cruise ship with an outbreak of the deadly disease has been ordered to remain in an Omaha facility for two more weeks. She says she tested negative.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Rebecca S. Gratz/Associated Press
item.media:description: The University of Nebraska Medical Center campus in Omaha houses the National Quarantine Unit, the only federally funded facility of its kind.

item.title: L.A. County Response to Altadena Fire Was Not Biased, Report Finds
item.description: An examination of the disaster commissioned by the county concluded that its efforts were muddled and chaotic, but said that it had not discriminated against Black residents.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nearly all of the 19 people who died in the Eaton fire lived in West Altadena.

item.title: What is Trump’s $1.776 Billion ‘Anti-Weaponization’ Fund?
item.description: The Trump administration is creating a $1.8 billion fund to compensate people it says were wronged by the federal government, a group that could be largely made up of the president’s allies.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department is tapping an unlimited fund created by Congress to settle lawsuits filed against the government.

item.title: Georgia Republicans Grasp for a Contender to Take On Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff
item.description: On the eve of their primary, Republicans have grown nervous about their prospects in November against Mr. Ossoff, regarded as the most vulnerable Democratic senator in the fall midterms.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Jon Ossoff, Democrat of Georgia, is running for re-election in a battleground state.

item.title: ICE Agent Charged in Shooting of a Venezuelan Immigrant in Minnesota
item.description: The agent is the second federal officer to face felony charges in Minnesota stemming from Operation Metro Surge, the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño and Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police blocked off an area in Minneapolis near the shooting on Jan. 14.

item.title: Northwestern Names Mung Chiang as President After Tumult Over Protests
item.description: The previous president resigned after months of conflict over how the university handled protests. The new president, Mung Chiang, currently leads Purdue.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Saul
item.media:credit: Kaiti Sullivan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mung Chiang is the current president of Purdue University. He will become Northwestern’s president this summer.

item.title: Abortion Pill Lawsuit Leaves Trump in a Political Bind Ahead of the Midterms
item.description: Louisiana wants the Food and Drug Administration to curtail access to the medication. Doing so could cost Republicans at the polls.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:credit: Drew Angerer/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A lawsuit against the F.D.A. threatens access to medication abortion, and the Trump administration has remained strikingly silent about the case, even as it reached the Supreme Court.

item.title: Keisha Lance Bottoms Was Atlanta’s Mayor in the Pandemic. Can She Win the Governor’s Race?
item.description: Keisha Lance Bottoms is the Democrats’ front-runner as the primary approaches. But her party rivals are trying to ensure she has to face a runoff.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas and Michelle Baruchman
item.media:credit: Brynn Anderson/Associated Press
item.media:description: Keisha Lance Bottoms arriving this month to vote early in the Georgia primary.

item.title: Justice Dept. Sets Up $1.8 Billion Fund That Could Funnel Money to Trump Allies
item.description: The arrangement was denounced by critics as a slush fund for supporters of President Trump, possibly including Jan. 6 rioters.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Andrew Duehren and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, two of his sons and his family business had argued in the case that the Internal Revenue Service should have done more to prevent a former contractor from leaking tax information.

item.title: Hegseth Campaigns for Trump Loyalist in Kentucky House Race
item.description: The campaign appearance was an extraordinary breach of military decorum, even for Mr. Hegseth, who has stretched the boundaries of partisan politics as defense secretary.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Pete Hegseth, the defense secretary, testifying at the Capitol last week.

item.title: G.O.P. to Begin Debating Voting Maps in This State. Not Everyone Approves.
item.description: Under pressure from President Trump, the South Carolina legislature will consider new House districts this week. Some Republicans worry changes could backfire.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Sean Rayford/Getty Images
item.media:description: This week’s special session of the South Carolina legislature is expected to be contentious.

item.title: Brookings Institution Report: Over 100,000 Family Separations in Trump Crackdown
item.description: The Brookings Institution suggests that federal statistics are an undercount because immigrant parents are not being asked about or not disclosing their American children.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan and Jeff Adelson
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Children have been separated from their parents, according to a new report, as the Trump administration continues its deportation push.

item.title: In Massie’s Kentucky Primary, Fidelity to Trump Is on the Ballot
item.description: Representative Thomas Massie, the Republican who wears his rifts with President Trump as badges of honor, is battling Trump-backed challenger Ed Gallrein to keep his seat.
item.dc:creator: Robert Draper
item.media:credit: Madeleine Hordinski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ed Gallrein campaigning last week in Simpsonville, Ky. A victory by Mr. Gallrein would be seen by other Republicans as evidence that President Trump’s hold over the party remains absolute.

item.title: Trump’s Approval Sinks Amid Unpopular War, Darkening G.O.P. Prospects
item.description: With the midterms nearing, President Trump’s approval rating has hit a second-term low as voters question his handling of the economy, according to the latest New York Times/Siena poll.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ashley Cai/The New York Times

item.title: The Supreme Court’s Voting Rights Decision Spurred Swift Action in Louisiana and Alabama
item.description: The timing brought the court into the middle of a fight to redraw voting maps across the South, even as some primaries were underway.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The timing of the Supreme Court’s decision has complicated voting maps in several states, even as some had begun their primary process.

item.title: He’s Been Impeached and Indicted. He Has a Chance in Texas’ Senate Race.
item.description: The fate of Ken Paxton, the conservative Texas firebrand, could decide whether Republicans keep control of the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kruse and Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
